coredata.dc:title: Green one-step synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots based on inner filter effect as fluorescent nanosensors for determination of Fe3+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jing
Guo, Yue
Qin, Linjun
Yue, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Qitu
Wang, Lixi
coredata.dc:description: Significant efforts need to be devoted to enhancing the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of carbon dots (CDs) due to the typically low PLQY (< 10%) of currently synthesized CDs. Moreover, certain special industries such as textiles, paper, and food all have high requirements for water since Fe3+ can affect its color, odor, and taste. The traditional detection methods require expensive equipment, so a facile method to detect Fe3+ is urgently needed. In this work, the self-doping of nitrogen (N-CDs) has been achieved through tea residues as the carbon source to inhibit the oxygen-deficient state, and the doping of boric acid (BN-CDs) can activate the electron transition at the carbon site, which effectively enhanced the PLQY of CDs from 11.4% to 25.79% compared to N-CDs. Furthermore, as hydroxylated polyphenols in tea residues can adsorb metal ions, stable non-fluorescent matrix state complexes have been established between BN-CDs and Fe3+ to quench the FL owing to the inner filter effect. The linear relationship was formed in the concentration range of 0–60 μM, and the detection limit reached 0.07 μM. It demonstrates that along with the effective treatment of waste tea residues, the synthesized high fluorescence property BN-CDs are suitable to detect Fe3+ in water for textiles, paper, food, and other special industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tea residues
Hydrothermal method
Boric acid
Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthesis process and reaction mechanism of BN-CDs; (b) The CIE chromaticity coordinates the diagram of BN-CDs and N-CDs; (c) The PLQY of CDs reported by different works.
(a–c) TEM images of BN-CDs; (e) XRD pattern of the synthesized BN-CDs and N-CDs; (d, f) TEM images of N-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum of BN-CDs and N-CDs; (b) XPS survey scans of BN-CDs, the inset is the atomic percentages of elements; The narrow spectrum of BN-CDs: (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, (f) B1s.

(a) Absorption, optimal excitation, and emission spectrum of BN-CDs, the inset is a photograph of BN-CD solution exposed to sunlight and 365 nm UV light, respectively; (b) FL emission spectrum of BN-CDs under diverse excitation wavelengths (290–490 nm); The factors affecting the FL intensity of BN-CDs: (c, d) pH values (λex = 370 nm); (e) storage time (λex = 370 nm); (f) concentration of NaCl (λex = 370 nm).

(a) Relationship among various metal ions and the effect of FL quenching to BN-CDs; (b) FL emission spectrum of BN-CDs within the existence of different metal ions (λex = 370 nm); (c) FL emission spectrum of BN-CDs under the existence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (0–500 μM); (d) Linear relationship between quenching efficiency of BN-CDs and Fe3+ concentration.
FL quenching mechanism between Fe3+ and BN-CDs.

(a) Absorption spectrum of Fe3+, optimal excitation and emission spectrum of BN-CDs; (b) absorption spectrum of Fe3+, BN-CDs, and BN-CDs/Fe3+; (c, d) FL lifetime decay curves of BN-CDs and BN-CDs/Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Green one-step synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots based on inner filter effect as fluorescent nanosensors for determination of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites, Nanjing, 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Significant efforts need to be devoted to enhancing the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of carbon dots (CDs) due to the typically low PLQY (< 10%) of currently synthesized CDs. Moreover, certain special industries such as textiles, paper, and food all have high requirements for water since Fe3+ can affect its color, odor, and taste. The traditional detection methods require expensive equipment, so a facile method to detect Fe3+ is urgently needed. In this work, the self-doping of nitrogen (N-CDs) has been achieved through tea residues as the carbon source to inhibit the oxygen-deficient state, and the doping of boric acid (BN-CDs) can activate the electron transition at the carbon site, which effectively enhanced the PLQY of CDs from 11.4% to 25.79% compared to N-CDs. Furthermore, as hydroxylated polyphenols in tea residues can adsorb metal ions, stable non-fluorescent matrix state complexes have been established between BN-CDs and Fe3+ to quench the FL owing to the inner filter effect. The linear relationship was formed in the concentration range of 0–60 μM, and the detection limit reached 0.07 μM. It demonstrates that along with the effective treatment of waste tea residues, the synthesized high fluorescence property BN-CDs are suitable to detect Fe3+ in water for textiles, paper, food, and other special industries.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) possess distinct advantages in color purity and size compared with rare earth-doping phosphors. Carbon quantum dots (Carbon Dots, CDs), a kind of novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (size ≤ 10 nm) with carbon as the basic element, have abundances of organic functional groups on their surface [1–3]. In contrast to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are already extensively applied in bioimaging, sensors, light catalysis, drug carrier, and tracer fields due to good photobleaching resistance, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and water solubility [4–8]. Nevertheless, the FL properties of CDs are easily affected by surface defects owing to the easy trapping of electrons and holes by these defects, such as decreasing PLQY and affecting their applications [9]. Fe3+ is considered to be one of the most common metal ions. For some special industries including textile, paper, food, and others that have high requirements for Fe3+ in water, a high concentration of Fe3+ can impact its color, smell, and taste. Currently, the commonly used detection methods mainly include voltammetry, spectrophotometry and atomic absorption spectroscopy, etc. These instruments are usually expensive, and the sample preparation process is also very complex. Hence, it is particularly necessary to establish a convenient method to detect Fe3+ [10–13].

The preparation methods of CDs cover the microwave method, pyrolysis method, hydrothermal method, etc. Compared to other methods, the hydrothermal method is simpler and more controllable, allowing oxidation and modification in a single step, and also avoiding the volatilization of toxic substances since the reaction is carried out in a closed reactor [14–17]. Since the electronegativity of N, S, B, and P atoms is different from that of C atoms, the doping of these heteroatoms can change the electron arrangement of CDs and produce new n- or p-type carriers and optimize the FL properties [9]. Biomass wastes usually contain a certain amount of nitrogen, sulfur, and silicon in addition to a large amount of carbon and oxygen, which can achieve self-doped and self-passivated without doping additional chemicals [18]. Hence, biomass wastes are considered a potential alternative feedstock for various chemical resources. Recently, many researchers have synthesized CDs that possess superior fluorescence properties from grass [19], orange peel [18], bamboo leaves [20], garlic [21], lemon juice [22], waste cigarette butts [23], and green tea [24]. R. Atchudan et al. [25] synthesized high fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) via the use of Phyllanthus acidus as raw material. The FNCDs have significant blue luminescence at 365 nm UV light, and the QY is about 14%. Fe3+ can be associated with the surface groups of FNCDs owing to the strong affinity between them, disrupting the radiation leap and leading to the fluorescence quenching of FNCDs. The FNCDs can serve as FL probes to detect Fe3+ from 2 to 25 μM. On account of the rapid growth of tea culture and the tea products industry, the annual consumption of tea worldwide is increasing in these years. The surface of the soaked tea residue is rich in amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which makes the CDs prepared from this raw material have good water solubility and are easier to functionalize [26–29]. Additionally, the waste tea residues contain a large number of tea polyphenols, whose chemical structure includes many hydroxy polyphenols that can form stable complexes with metal ions. Thus, the CDs prepared with waste tea residues are useable to detect metal ions.

Herein, BN-CDs possessing distinct blue luminescence were produced by hydrothermal treatment with waste tea residues as the carbon and nitrogen sources, and boric acid as the boron source, respectively. The co-doping of elements was well achieved in a one-step hydrothermal reaction, and the particle size of the prepared CDs was relatively small. Such residual components as theanine in tea residues provide the CDs with self-doping of nitrogen, which can suppress the oxygen defect state on its surface and improve the FL performance. And boron element has one less valence electron than the carbon element, so the introduction of boron generates the p-type carriers inside the BN-CDs to achieve an enhancement in QY from 11.4% to 25.79% by changing the electronic structure. Moreover, BN-CDs can also form stable complexes with Fe3+ ions, and the electrons of BN-CDs in partially excited states will fill the 3d orbitals of Fe3+, causing the FL quenching of BN-CDs as a consequence of the inner filter effect. The BN-CDs can serve as probes to detect Fe3+ selectively in a well linear relationship (0–60 μM) between quenching efficiency and Fe3+ concentration, and the relevant lower limit of detection reaches 0.07 μM. It provides a simple method for Fe3+ detection by the prepared CDs while alleviating the degradation pressure on the environment caused by excessive waste tea residues.

Tieguanyin tea leaves (Bama Tea Company), Boric acid (AR, Yonghua Chemical Company), Dialysis bag (MW1000, Yibo Biology), Needle filter (0.22 μm, Jinteng). The chemical reagents used for metal ions detection are all chloride (Table S1).

First of all, 1.1 g of dry Tieguanyin tea residues were mixed with 0.05 M boric acid solution (20 mL) and blended for 5 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave with a PTFE liner (50 mL) and reaction at 220 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed and the reactor cooled to room temperature, the reaction material was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the black deposit. The upper layer of solution was purged through a filter membrane (0.22 μm) and then dialyzed for 24 h to eliminate impurities (MW 1000). The partially purified BN-CDs were freeze-dried into a powder, and the other part was kept at 4 °C. In this experiment, the preparation conditions including the concentration of the boric acid solution, temperature, and time were all optimized. In addition, N-CDs were prepared without the doped of boric acid to analyze the influence of boric acid addition on the fluorescence properties of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectrums were all acquired by Lumina 222–26300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The absorption spectrums were acquired by a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray diffraction spectrum in the range of 15°∼75° was recorded on the D/Max2500 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). Topography of BN-CDs viewed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200s, USA). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) of BN-CDs observed with Thermo IN10 (Thermo Fisher, USA). The elemental composition of BN-CDs was tested via X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, Thermo Fisher K-alpha, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes tested by steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK).

The quantum yields (Qx) of N-CDs and BN-CDs were measured based on the following formula considering quinine sulfate (λex = 350 nm, Qs = 54%) in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution as the criteria:
(1)
Q x
=
Q s
×
I x
I s
×
A s
A x
×
(
η x
η s
)
2
where the subscript s represents the standard sample, and x represents the analyte. Q, I, η and A denote QY, emission peak integration area, refractive index, and absorbance, respectively.

After mixing 3 mL of BN-CDs solution with 2 mL of Fe3+ ion solution at a concentration of 1 mM and blending for 5 min. The FL emission spectrums of the mixture over the range of 350–650 nm (λex = 370 nm) were then recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Adding other metal ions solutions to the BN-CDs solution namely Na+, Co2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Li+, Ca2+, K+, and Zn2+ ions were also recorded according to the above method.

To detect the sensitivity of BN-CDs to Fe3+ selection, the Fe3+ solution with a concentration of 1 mM was used as the master solution and diluted to 0–500 μM. Combining 1 mL of BN-CDs solution to 3 mL of various concentrations of Fe3+ solution and then blending for 5 min. The FL emission spectrums were collected using the same conditions as above, and the limit of detection for Fe3+ in the sensing system was calculated with the following formula:
(2)
L O D =
3 σ
κ
in which σ denotes the standard deviation on the FL intensity of blank samples in this work, and κ denotes the slope of the fitted line.

By using recycled waste tea residues as raw material and boric acid as a dopant, BN-CDs were obtained via hydrothermal treatment, and the specific synthesis steps and reaction mechanism are illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The effects of boric acid concentration, temperature, and time to FL intensity of the BN-CDs are portrayed in Figs. S1a–c. The strongest FL intensity of BN-CDs obtained via hydrothermal reaction at 220 °C for 6 h occurred with a boric acid concentration of 0.05 M. Tea residues lose moisture and are carbonized during the hydrothermal process, and the reaction temperature affects the degree of carbonization for the biomass. The surface defects of BN-CDs are temperature-dependent, more oxygen-containing functional groups will be formed as the temperature rises higher and the FL intensity increases accordingly. However, if the temperature is too high, the excessive heating of BN-CDs particles can destroy their surface structures and the fluorescence emission gets reduced [30]. The influence of time on the FL intensity is similar to that of temperature for CDs. The position of the emission peaks of BN-CDs did not change significantly with the preparation conditions, suggesting that the particle size of BN-CDs was not noticeably affected.

Quantum yield (QY) is an important measure of the FL performance of CDs, and it is closely related to the FL intensity of CDs [25]. To further demonstrate that the incorporation of boric acid can improve the FL properties of CDs, the CDs without doping were hydrothermally reacted at different temperatures for 6 h along with the same other conditions. As demonstrated in Fig. S1d, the FL intensities of BN-CDs are all significantly higher than those of N-CDs. The QY of BN-CDs increases from 11.4% to 25.79% compared with N-CDs, and the color purity of BN-CDs is remarkably superior to that of N-CDs (Fig. 1b). By comparing the carbon dots prepared in this work with other reported in recent years, Fig. 1c indicates that the QY of BN-CDs is much higher than other reported CDs (specific information is shown in Table S2) [9,20,21,24,31,32]. The mechanism involved in BN-CDs consists of the first step of hydrolysis of tea residues, followed by small-stage condensation [23,33]. Among them, the self-doping of nitrogen to suppress defective states on the surface of carbon dots can be achieved in the one-step reaction due to the use of biomass waste as feedstock. New oxygen-, nitrogen- and boron-containing groups are also generated on account of the presence of boric acid. The boron is successfully doped by nucleophilic substitution of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots, and the fluorescence properties are enhanced by rearranging the electrons of the carbon dots [34].

BN-CDs exhibit excellent dispersion in aqueous solutions and are sphere-like particles in shape (Fig. 2 a). By counting 50 particles with uniform and narrow particle size distribution in Fig. 2c and f, the calculated average particle size from the histogram in the inset was 3.65 ± 0.75 nm for BN-CDs and 2.99 ± 0.72 nm for N-CDs. The distinct lattice stripes can be observed from the HRTEM images (Fig. 2b and d), where the lattice spacing of BN-CDs (0.315 nm) is marginally larger than that of N-CDs (0.301 nm), but both belong to the (002) plane of the graphite structure [35,36]. The obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.22° and 2θ = 28.38° in the XRD patterns can be indexed to BN-CDs and N-CDs (Fig. 2e), demonstrating that both of them have excellent crystallinity [37]. The peak position of BN-CDs is slightly shifted to a small angle compared to N-CDs, indicating that the lattice constants of BN-CDs become larger, which is in line with the analysis above. The results mentioned above revealed that the prepared CDs have a graphitic structure and the successful doping of boron atoms leads to the increase of particle and structural spacing of CDs.

The surface groups of CDs were analyzed by FTIR tests. Fig. 3 a illustrates the FTIR of BN-CDs and N-CDs. There were significant peaks of BN-CDs at 3430, 2930, 1640, 1420, 1100, and 766 cm−1, which are linked to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H, C–H, C=O, B–O, B–C, and the bending vibration of B–N, respectively [38–42]. While N-CDs have distinct peaks at 3430, 2940, 1650, 1410, and 1130 cm−1, which are associated with the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, C–H, C=O, C–N, and C–N/C–O, respectively [23,38–41,43]. The existence of these peaks confirms the self-doping of the N element and the successful incorporation of the B element in BN-CDs.

The elemental components of CDs were analyzed by XPS tests. The prepared BN-CDs were primarily consisted of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and boron elements (74.15%, 4.28%, 21.45%, and 0.13% elemental percentages, respectively), as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3b. However, the X-ray photoelectron spectrum of BN-CDs has three distinct peaks at 284.47 eV (C1s), 531.80 eV (O1s), and 399.40 eV (N1s). The peak of B1s is not obvious due to the small amount of boron doping, but the partially enlarged figure shows that B1s has a peak at 191.11 eV. There are three evident peaks in the narrow spectrum of C1s at 284.66 eV, 286.04 eV, and 288.39 eV, which belong to the C–C, C–O, and O–C=O groups, respectively (Fig. 3c) [42,44]. The narrow spectrum of N1s has two distinct peaks at 399.79 eV and 400.28 eV, which are associated with the N of pyrrole and (C3)–N group (Fig. 3d) [42,45,46]. The existence of pyrrole N indicates that N is distributed at the edge of the BN-CDs framework. There are two noticeable peaks in the narrow spectrum of O1s at 531.55 eV and 532.22 eV, which respond to the C=O and C–O groups (Fig. 3e) [40]. Fig. 3f descripts that the narrow spectrum of B1s presents two visible peaks at 189.98 eV and 191.90 eV, which correlate with those of the B–C and B–C2O groups [42]. The above results indicate the successful incorporation of boron into CDs. Moreover, the surfaces of the synthesized BN-CDs possess substantial amounts of oxygen- and amine-containing groups, suggesting that BN-CDs have good water solubility [23,26].

The BN-CDs aqueous solution is pale yellow in sunlight and emits brilliant blue fluorescence upon irradiation via 365 nm UV light as depicted in the inset of Fig. 4 a. The absorption spectrum of BN-CDs demonstrates significant shoulder peaks at around 285 nm and 335 nm which are assigned to π-π* conversion of the sp2-hybridized aromatic carbon and n-π* conversion of the C=O bond, respectively (Fig. 4a). It reveals that BN-CDs contain lone pairs of electrons on their surface, including carboxyl, amino, and amide groups. They form bonds with sp2-hybridized aromatics, leading to the transition of electrons from n-to the π-energy state between the functional group and the aromatic group [47–50]. In addition, the BN-CDs solution did not exhibit obvious fluorescence emission while being excited at 285 nm, indicating that the aromatic π system is not an effective emission center (Fig. 4b). BN-CDs have the optimal excitation wavelength situated at 368 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength located at 438 nm as depicted in Fig. 4a. There exists a progressive red-shift along with the increasing of excitation wavelength (290–490 nm) for the emission peak position of BN-CDs, which indicates that BN-CDs possess excitation wavelength dependence. This can be assigned to the quantum effects of BN-CDs or the diverse emission points on the surface. The excitation wavelength influences the energy distribution, charge splitting, electron and hole binding, and radiative reorganization of quantum dots in the excited state [51]. This property allows BN-CDs to be applied in bioimaging fields.

Fig. 4c and d depict the impact of pH values on the FL intensity of BN-CDs. The FL intensity of BN-CDs aqueous solution varied significantly with the pH value, and the strongest FL intensity was observed at the pH value is 4. And the FL of BN-CDs is considerably quenched under strong acidic and alkaline environments, probably because there exists a significant amount of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of BN-CDs. Moreover, excess H+ or OH− can also cause remarkable changes to the functional groups, thus destroying or inhibiting the electron conversion of certain defects and eventually resulting in the weakening of the FL intensity of carbon dots [52]. Regarding the response mechanism of CDs to pH value, the current conjectures mainly include protonation and deprotonation, energy level change, and aggregation of CDs [23,53,54]. The result reveals that the prepared BN-CDs exhibited remarkable sensitivity to pH values.

The impact of storage time (tested every 3 days) upon the FL intensity of aqueous BN-CDs solutions is depicted in Fig. 4e. Where F represents the FL intensity of BN-CDs aqueous solution on day n (n = 0, 3, 6, …, 30), F0 represents the FL intensity of initial BN-CDs aqueous solution, and F/F0 reflects the FL intensity of BN-CDs changes over time. The above results suggest that the FL intensity of BN-CDs aqueous solution kept more than 80% of the original one without much change within 30 days. The impact that salt concentration has on the FL intensity of BN-CDs is demonstrated in Fig. 4f, where F and F0 represent the FL intensity of BN-CDs in NaCl solution with various concentrations and aqueous solution, respectively, and F/F0 reflects the salt resistance of BN-CDs. It revealed that the FL intensity of BN-CDs with the increasing concentration of NaCl solution (0–200 mM) changed inapparent. It is observed that BN-CDs have a high salt tolerance, which makes them suitable for biochemical analysis and detection. To investigate the photostability of BN-CDs. The prepared BN-CDs were irradiated under UV light for 0–120 min, and their FL intensity was tested every 10 min (Fig. S2). The FL of BN-CDs quenching by the UV light greatly within the initial 10 min since the conjugated π-electron structure in the fabric of BN-CDs is easily destroyed by light. However, the FL of BN-CDs was maintained above 75% in the subsequent light experiments due to the presence of numerous carboxyl groups on their surface, indicating that BN-CDs have relatively good photostability [23]. Overall, the prepared BN-CDs demonstrate good stability and applicability in the sensor field [4,5,55].

To determine whether BN-CDs have selective fluorescence sensing for Fe3+, eleven common metal ions were selected as test objects for fluorescence quenching properties in this work (the concentrations of all metal ions were kept at 1 mM). Fig. 5 a illustrates the efficiency of FL quenching of BN-CDs by various metal ions, containing Fe3+, Na+, Co2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Li+, Ca2+, K+, and Zn2+. Where F represents the FL intensity of BN-CDs after adding metal ions, F0 stands for the FL intensity of the blank sample, and F/F0 reflects the FL quenching effect. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectrum exhibits in Fig. 5b. It is possible to form stable non-fluorescent matrix state complexes between BN-CDs and Fe3+ on account of the strong affinity between them, implying that a high degree of quenching of BN-CDs fluorescence can be achieved by Fe3+ [56,57]. Thus, Fe3+ adsorbs on the surface sites of BN-CDs by coordinating with the amino and carboxyl groups on their surfaces [21]. The above results graphically demonstrate that the BN-CDs nanosensor exhibits a stronger selection of Fe3+ than other metal ions.

By measuring the variation in FL intensity of BN-CDs upon the existence of different concentrations of Fe3+ to explore the detection range of BN-CDs for Fe3+. BN-CDs displayed a significant fluorescence sensitivity to the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 5c), indicating the FL intensity of BN-CDs progressively decreased as an increase of Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 500 μM in the solution. The FL quenching of BN-CDs is up to 62% when the concentration of Fe3+ ions reaches 500 μM (Fig. 5d). There exists a well linear regression of the quenching efficiency over the range of 0–60 μM for BN-CDs with the concentration of Fe3+ (inset of Fig. 5d), and the relevance is described as follows: F/F0 = 101.2499–0.42087 × CFe 3+ (μM), and the correlation factor R2 is 0.95566. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) is computed as 0.07 μM by following the 3σ rule, which is lower than other reported values attributed to the fact that Fe3+ can form complexes by stable chelation with the hydroxylated polyphenols present in the tea residues [9,21,25,58,59] (Table S3). These results revealed that the synthetic BN-CDs have a broad range of detection capabilities, and can contribute to the selective quenching of Fe3+ in water for textiles, paper, food, and other special industries.

The possible mechanism of quenching by Fe3+ towards BN-CDs was analyzed thoroughly. The absorption spectra of BN-CDs/Fe3+ is quite diverse from that of Fe3+ and BN-CDs as presented in Fig. 7 b, implying that the FL quenching of BN-CDs may occur owing to static adsorption. This work uses waste tea residues as raw materials, which contain a large number of residual tea polyphenols consisting of hydroxy polyphenols. Therefore, the synthesized BN-CDs contain organic groups on their surfaces, including phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, which are capable of interacting with metal ions to stable non-fluorescent matrix state complexes [26,56,57]. It makes the electrons of excited states transfer from the surface of BN-CDs towards the unfilled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ (Fig. 6) [60], which ultimately causes the FL quenching of BN-CDs. The absorption spectra of Fe3+ solution overlap well with the excitation spectra of BN-CDs and only partially with the emission spectra as demonstrated in Fig. 7a, thereby indicating that the FL quenching is likely elicited by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescence lifetimes of BN-CDs are 3.41 ns and 3.24 ns before and after the incorporation of Fe3+ (Fig. 7c and d), which are not significantly changed. This confirmed that the FL quenching of BN-CDs by Fe3+ is induced by IFE and there is no FRET between them [23].

To summarize, BN-CDs with distinct blue luminescence were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal treatment based on dried waste tea residues as carbon and nitrogen sources, and boric acid as boron sources. The doping of boric acid activated the electronic transition of CDs and improved the FL properties of BN-CDs with a maximum QY of 25.79%. Fe3+ could substantially quench the FL of BN-CDs on account of IFE. The FL quenching efficiency of BN-CDs displayed a well linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 60 μM, and its lower limit of detection was 0.07 μM. This work used waste tea residues as raw material, which not only realized the high value-added transformation of waste, but also effectively relieved the environmental pressure, and offers a novel approach to detect Fe3+ in water for textiles, paper, food, and other special industries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51202111), China Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), China and “Qinglan Project” Young and Middle-aged Academic Leaders Program of Jiangsu Province, China.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2022.10.253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-Dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Accounts Chem. Res.
